title: Savings accounts & interest: park it right
description: Put your money where it's safe, insured, and quietly growing.
summaryPoints: Explain interest both ways: banks pay savers and charge borrowers
Match the account to the goal: regular, CD, money market, or online
Check for FDIC insurance — up to $250,000 per depositor, per bank
Pick CD terms that mature BEFORE you need the money

content.title: The shoebox problem
content.body:
Stash your cash in a shoebox and it's safe from exactly one thing: you spending it. It earns nothing — and thanks to **inflation** (prices creeping up), every year that same pile buys a little less.

A savings account flips the script: the bank pays YOU for parking money there. That payment is called **interest**, and choosing the right account decides how hard your money works while it sits.

content.title: Interest flows two ways
content.body:
Interest is a two-way street, and you want to be on the receiving end:
- When you **save**, the bank pays you interest for holding your money.
- When you **borrow**, you pay the bank interest for using theirs.

The basic math is friendly: park $100 in an account paying 2% a year, and twelve months later you have $102. Not life-changing — yet. The real magic shows up later in this lesson.

content.instructions: One word flips this sentence wrong. Tap it.
content.sentence: Banks reward borrowers with interest for keeping money parked in savings.
content.explanation: Savers get paid interest; borrowers pay it. Which side of that flow you're on changes everything.

content.prompt: Park $100 at 2% yearly interest. After one year you have $___.
content.explanation: 2% of $100 is $2, so $100 + $2 = $102. Small — but the shoebox paid you $0.

content.title: Anytime money vs. locked money
content.body:
**Regular savings account** — open one almost anywhere, deposit or withdraw anytime, earn a little interest. The default home for an emergency fund.

**Certificate of Deposit (CD)** — you promise the bank your money for a set term (3 months, 1 year, 5 years...) and earn a higher rate in return. Break the deal early and you pay a penalty. Golden rule: pick a term that ends BEFORE you'll need the money.

content.prompt: Taking money out of a CD before its term ends usually costs you a penalty.
content.explanation: That's the trade: a higher rate in exchange for locking it up. Match the term to your timeline and you'll never pay it.

content.title: Two more parking spots
content.body:
**Money market account** — wants a higher minimum balance, pays better interest than regular savings, and still lets you reach the money (with some limits). Good for bigger balances you'd only touch in a real emergency.

**Online savings account** — no branches means lower costs for the bank, which usually means the best rates of the bunch. Runs entirely from an app. Always compare rates before picking: online banks often win.

content.prompt: Match each account to its superpower.

pairs.right: Higher rate, locked until a date

content.prompt: Pick the best parking spot for each situation.

items.text: Emergency fund you might need tomorrow
Tuition due in exactly one year
First $50 from a new part-time job
Gift money untouched until graduation in 2 years
Big savings you'd only tap in a true emergency

content.context: You're saving for college tuition that's due in exactly 2 years.
options: A 5-year CD — longest term, highest rate
A savings account, or a CD that matures within the 2 years
Your checking account, next to spending money
content.explanation: A CD works for a 2-year goal IF the term ends before the bill arrives — a 5-year lock means penalties. Checking leaks, and backpacks get lost.

content.title: The $250,000 safety net
content.body:
What if the bank itself goes under? In the U.S., that's what the **FDIC** (Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation) is for: if an insured bank fails, your money is protected up to **$250,000 per depositor, per bank**.

Your shoebox offers no such deal. Neither does your sock drawer, your wallet, or your cousin's "totally safe" app. Look for FDIC insurance before you open any account.

content.prompt: If an insured bank fails, the FDIC protects your money up to $___,000 per depositor, per bank.
content.explanation: Up to $250,000 per depositor, per insured bank. Boring, official — and exactly why banks beat shoeboxes.

content.title: Compound interest: the snowball
content.body:
Here's the magic: **compound interest** means the interest you earn starts earning interest of its own.

$100 at 5% a year:
- Year 1: $100 + $5 = **$105**
- Year 2: 5% of $105 is $5.25 → **$110.25**

Each year the snowball is a little bigger, so each year it picks up more snow. Slow at first, unstoppable later — the longer you leave it rolling, the harder it works.
content.imageAlt: Snowball rolling downhill, growing larger as it goes

content.prompt: Put the compound-interest snowball in motion — order the chain.
steps: The bank pays interest on your $100
Next year's interest is calculated on the bigger balance
content.explanation: Interest joins the pile, then the whole pile earns more interest. That loop is the entire trick.

content.prompt: You park $100 at 5% compound interest and ignore it for 10 years. Where does it land?
content.explanation: Simple interest would add $5 × 10 = $50. Compounding gets you to about $163, because every year's interest starts earning too.

content.title: Park It Right
content.intro: Same money, different parking spots. Two years on the clock — collect interest, dodge penalties, and don't get scammed.

steps.situation: Your $200 emergency fund needs a home — and you might need it on short notice.

options.feedback: Insured, reachable in a day, and the rate beats the branch down the street. Emergency money should earn while it waits.

options.text: A shoebox labeled DO NOT OPEN
options.feedback: Interest earned: zero. And inflation quietly shrank what that $200 can buy.

options.text: A 5-year CD for the juicy rate
options.feedback: Then your bike tire blew in March. The early-withdrawal penalty ate the extra interest — and then some.

steps.situation: A $300 tuition payment is due in exactly one year.

options.text: A 1-year CD that matures the week before the bill
options.feedback: Term matches timeline: better rate, zero chance of an early-exit penalty.

options.text: Checking account, next to your spending money
options.feedback: It earned nothing — and $40 of it mysteriously became bubble tea. Separate accounts protect goals.
You had to break it at month 12, and the penalty wiped out the rate advantage.

steps.situation: Your savings have grown into serious money. You want it growing — but reachable if life swings.

options.feedback: Higher interest than regular savings, still reachable in an emergency. That's exactly its job.

options.text: Leave it all in regular savings
options.feedback: Totally safe — but at this balance, a money market would pay you noticeably more for the same safety.
Zero interest, zero FDIC protection, and inflation took a bite. The mattress always loses.

steps.situation: An app ad screams: "GUARANTEED 20% monthly returns! Not a bank — no boring rules or insurance!"

options.text: Hard pass — your money stays FDIC-insured
options.feedback: Boring is a feature. 'Guaranteed' high returns with no insurance is the oldest scam recipe there is.
Too good to be true means it isn't true. With no FDIC, nobody owes you that money back.

options.text: Move just half, to be safe
options.feedback: Half a scam is still a scam. 'Diversifying' into a con just shrinks the con's paperwork.

content.outro: Right account, right timeline, FDIC on the door. Parked like that, money doesn't just sit — it works.
